metadata.summary: Metrics for measuring LLM performance across accuracy, quality, latency, cost, safety, and reliability dimensions.

content.title: Evaluation Metrics
content.summary: Metrics for measuring LLM performance across different dimensions including accuracy, quality, latency, cost, safety, and reliability. Select 3-5 metrics matching the task type, run them against a fixed test dataset, compare to a pre-established baseline, and report pass/fail per metric against defined thresholds.

why.p: LLM evaluation requires quantifiable metrics across multiple dimensions to detect regressions, compare model versions, and gate production rollouts. Without a baseline and structured metric selection, absolute scores have no meaning and quality changes go undetected. Automated metrics combined with human spot-checking provide the most reliable signal across accuracy, safety, latency, and cost.

list.item: Implementing automated quality gates in a CI/CD pipeline for LLM-powered features.
Comparing two model versions or prompt variants before a production rollout.
Tracking metric trends over time in a production monitoring dashboard.
Building an evaluation harness that a subagent can run autonomously against a test dataset.
Selecting the right metric for a specific task type (classification, QA, summarization, code generation).
The task has no quantifiable success criterion — open-ended creative generation requires human preference evaluation, not automated metrics.
Ground-truth labels are unavailable and cannot be synthesized with validation; BLEU/ROUGE without references are meaningless.
Exact match or BLEU are the only metrics for long-form generation — they punish valid paraphrases and correlate poorly with human judgment.
Safety metrics (toxicity, bias) are the sole gate for production decisions — automated safety metrics miss context-dependent harms and require human review.

section.name: evaluation-dimensions
section.p: Metrics for measuring LLM performance across different dimensions including accuracy, quality, latency, cost, safety, and reliability.

list.item: Quality — Accuracy, F1, BLEU, ROUGE — Priority: High
Latency — p50, p95, p99 — Priority: Medium-High
Cost — $/1K tokens, $/request — Priority: Medium
Safety — Toxicity, bias, refusal rate — Priority: High
Reliability — Success rate, error rate — Priority: High

section.name: common-metrics
section.p: Core metric implementations for exact match, contains match, length ratio, BLEU, ROUGE, and semantic similarity.

example.title: Exact Match
Contains Match
Length Ratio
BLEU Score
ROUGE Score
Semantic Similarity

section.name: task-specific-metrics
section.p: Task-specific metric implementations for classification, question answering, summarization, and code generation.

example.title: Classification Metrics
Question Answering Metrics
example.code:
def qa_exact_match(prediction: str, ground_truth: str) -> float: """QA exact match after normalization.""" import re import string
def normalize(text): # Remove articles, punctuation, extra whitespace text = text.lower() text = re.sub(r'\b(a|an|the)\b', ' ', text) text = text.translate(str.maketrans('', '', string.punctuation)) text = ' '.join(text.split()) return text
return 1.0 if normalize(prediction) == normalize(ground_truth) else 0.0
def qa_f1(prediction: str, ground_truth: str) -> float: """Token-level F1 for QA.""" pred_tokens = prediction.lower().split() truth_tokens = ground_truth.lower().split()
common = set(pred_tokens) & set(truth_tokens)
if len(common) == 0: return 0.0
precision = len(common) / len(pred_tokens) recall = len(common) / len(truth_tokens)
return 2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall)

example.title: Summarization Metrics
Code Generation Pass@k

section.name: performance-metrics
section.p: Performance metrics for latency statistics, cost calculation, and reliability tracking.

example.title: Latency Statistics
example.code:
import numpy as np
def latency_stats(latencies: List[float]) -> Dict: """Calculate latency statistics.""" return { "mean_ms": np.mean(latencies), "median_ms": np.median(latencies), "p50_ms": np.percentile(latencies, 50), "p95_ms": np.percentile(latencies, 95), "p99_ms": np.percentile(latencies, 99), "min_ms": np.min(latencies), "max_ms": np.max(latencies) }

example.title: Cost Metrics
Reliability Metrics

section.name: safety-metrics
section.p: Safety metrics for toxicity detection, bias detection, and refusal rate measurement.

example.title: Toxicity Detection
Bias Detection
Refusal Rate

section.name: aggregation
section.p: Metric aggregation utilities for combining results across a test dataset and producing summary statistics.

example.title: Aggregate Single Metric
Multi-Metric Summary

section.name: limitations
section.p: Known failure modes and limitations of automated evaluation metrics.

list.item: BLEU scores below 0.3 are unreliable discriminators between models — differences of 0.01-0.02 BLEU have no practical significance.
semantic_similarity using cosine of embeddings can score near 1.0 for antonyms if the embedding space collapses similar topics ("good" vs. "not good").
refusal_rate using string patterns is brittle — models change refusal phrasing with version updates, causing false negatives.
classification_f1 with average='macro' penalizes imbalanced datasets unfairly; use 'weighted' for production traffic distributions.
The code_pass_at_k metric requires runnable test cases — agents cannot generate reliable test cases without domain knowledge validation.
Latency stats computed from a small sample (fewer than 30 requests) are misleading for p95/p99 — these require 100+ samples to stabilize.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: An agent using evaluation metrics should select 3-5 metrics matching the task type, run them against a fixed test dataset using the aggregate_metrics pattern, compare results to a pre-established baseline, and report pass/fail per metric against defined thresholds. For production monitoring, the agent samples live traffic, applies lightweight automated checks, and escalates to LLM-as-judge only for flagged samples to control cost.

Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent (executes metric-selection and evaluation-run tasks from an SDD task card); an evaluation subagent (custom — takes a test dataset path, model endpoint, and metric list; returns a structured JSON summary using create_metric_summary).

content.prompt: Given this task type: {classification|qa|summarization|generation}, select the 3 most appropriate metrics from: [exact_match, contains_match, bleu_score, rouge_score, semantic_similarity, classification_f1, qa_f1, latency_stats, refusal_rate, safe_content_rate]. Return JSON: {"metrics": [str], "rationale": str, "thresholds": {"metric": float}}

Evaluate the following {N} model outputs against ground truth. Apply metrics: {metric_list}. Return: {"pass": bool, "summary": {metric: {mean, min, max}}, "failures": [case_idx]} where pass=true only if all metric means exceed their thresholds.

section.name: task-metric-selector
section.p: Task-type metric selector with threshold defaults for the four primary task categories.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: CLI tools and libraries for metric computation.

list.item: evaluate (HuggingFace) — BLEU, ROUGE, accuracy, F1 in one library — pip install evaluate / huggingface.co/docs/evaluate
rouge-score — ROUGE-1/2/L for summarization — pip install rouge-score
nltk — BLEU, tokenization — pip install nltk / nltk.org
scikit-learn — F1, accuracy, classification report — pip install scikit-learn / scikit-learn.org
sentence-transformers — Semantic similarity via embeddings — pip install sentence-transformers
perspective (Perspective API) — Toxicity scoring — perspectiveapi.com (requires API key)
numpy — Latency stats (percentiles) — pip install numpy

section.name: services
section.p: Evaluation services and platforms with agent-friendly APIs.

list.item: Langfuse — SaaS/OSS (MIT) — Yes, SDK + REST API — Store and query metric scores per trace.
Braintrust — SaaS — Yes, evaluation SDK — Built-in metric library, dataset versioning.
RAGAS — OSS — Yes, Python library — RAG-specific metrics (faithfulness, context precision).
OpenAI Evals — OSS — Yes, YAML/Python eval definitions — Standardized eval runner; good for regression testing.
Arize Phoenix — SaaS/OSS — Yes, phoenix.evals module — LLM-as-judge evaluation with structured criteria.
HuggingFace evaluate — OSS — Yes, programmatic API — 100+ metrics, reproducible implementations.
Perspective API — SaaS — Yes, REST — Toxicity scores with attribute breakdown.

rule.statement: Use multiple metrics (3-5) per task type rather than relying on a single score.
rule.rationale: No single metric captures all quality dimensions; combining metrics reduces blind spots and gaming.

rule.statement: Choose metrics that match the evaluation goal for the specific task type.
rule.rationale: Task-mismatched metrics produce misleading results — BLEU is appropriate for translation but not open-ended generation.

rule.statement: Combine automated metrics with a 5-10% human spot-check sample.
rule.rationale: Automated metrics can plateau while human-judged quality degrades (or vice versa).

rule.statement: Always establish a baseline metric run against the current production model before evaluating a candidate.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline, absolute numbers have no meaning and cannot indicate improvement or regression.

rule.statement: Use at least 100 test cases for p95 latency and metric means to be statistically stable.
rule.rationale: Fewer than 30 cases produces results with confidence intervals too wide to act on.

rule.statement: Track metrics over time in a time-series store (Langfuse, W&B).
rule.rationale: A single evaluation snapshot is much less valuable than a trend showing metric drift over deployments.

rule.statement: For multi-label classification, always report per-class F1 alongside macro/weighted.
rule.rationale: A high weighted F1 can hide a complete failure on rare classes that matter for production correctness.

rule.statement: Weight output token cost in latency analysis — separate TTFT from total latency in streaming APIs.
rule.rationale: Long outputs inflate wall-clock time; TTFT (time to first token) is the user-perceived latency signal for streaming.

rule.statement: Refusal rate thresholds depend on the prompt type — for safety-critical prompts a 95% refusal rate is correct, not a failure.
rule.rationale: Context-blind thresholds produce false positives for intentionally restrictive safety gates.

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: AI-agent-specific gotchas when implementing evaluation metric pipelines.

list.item: BLEU and ROUGE use n-gram overlap — agents should not interpret a 0.01 difference as meaningful; these metrics require large test sets and aggregate interpretation.
semantic_similarity requires a consistent embedding model across runs; switching embedding models between baseline and candidate invalidates the comparison.
Latency benchmarks run from an agent loop include agent overhead (API call latency, parsing time) — isolate model latency by calling the model endpoint directly, not through an agent wrapper.
The safe_content_rate function loads unitary/toxic-bert on first call — agents running in serverless environments will time out on cold start; pre-warm or use the Perspective API instead.
Cost metrics require accurate token counts — agents that estimate tokens from character count (divided by 4) rather than tokenizer output will have 15-30% error on non-English text.

section.name: agent-selection
section.p: Recommended model selection by task for evaluation workflows.

list.item: Metric selection — sonnet — Evaluation approach requires reasoning about task objectives and metric properties.
Baseline establishment — sonnet — Reference setting for comparison requires understanding of the evaluation context.
Threshold definition — sonnet — Criteria setting requires domain knowledge and understanding of acceptable ranges.

checklist.check: Define objectives and success metrics before selecting evaluation approach.
Review task requirements to identify applicable metric families.
Plan evaluation approach: dataset size, metric selection, baseline model.
Implement core metric functions or select from existing libraries.
Test metric implementation against known examples with expected outputs.
Validate metric selection against requirements and task type.
Establish baseline run against current production model.
Run evaluation against candidate model using aggregate_metrics pattern.
Optimize evaluation pipeline based on results (batching, caching, sampling).
Evaluate results against thresholds — report pass/fail per metric.
Document learnings and threshold calibration decisions.
Set up time-series tracking for ongoing production monitoring.

section.name: templates
section.p: Strategy and implementation templates for evaluation metric workflows.
section.code:
# Evaluation Metrics Strategy
## Overview [Brief description of approach]
## Key Metrics - [Metric 1] - [Metric 2] - [Metric 3]
## Timeline - Phase 1: [When] - Phase 2: [When] - Phase 3: [When]
## Success Criteria - [Criteria 1] - [Criteria 2]

## Step 1: [Action] - [ ] Prerequisite 1 - [ ] Prerequisite 2 - [ ] Execute action - [ ] Measure results
## Step 2: [Action] - [ ] Prerequisite 1 - [ ] Prerequisite 2 - [ ] Execute action - [ ] Measure results
| Week | Status | Metric | Result | Notes | |------|--------|--------|--------|-------| | 1 | [In Progress] | | | | | 2 | [In Progress] | | | | | 3 | [Completed] | | | |
